text: The video focuses on a central tumor located adjacent to the renal artery, highlighting its proximity to this major blood vessel.
The surgeon examines a small tumor during an off-clamp robotic partial nephrectomy, focusing on its size and location relative to the renal structures.
The video begins with a view of a large renal tumor, highlighting its size and position relative to surrounding anatomical structures.
The video displays a large renal mass, prominently visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon points out the large mass located at the top of the surgical field, which is visually prominent in the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the kidney located below the large mass, highlighting its position relative to the tumor.
The spleen is visible in the background on the left side of the surgical field.
The surgeon clamps the renal artery, which is visibly isolated from surrounding tissue in preparation for nephrectomy.
The surgeon applies a clamp to the renal artery, visibly halting blood flow into the left kidney.
The surgeon has removed the surrounding fat from the kidney, preparing to flip the organ for further manipulation.
The kidney is flipped 180 degrees from its normal position, allowing the surgeon to begin resecting it.
The surgeon uses cold scissors to resect the kidney tissue, carefully cutting around a large, exophytic mass that protrudes outward from the kidney, allowing for a more superficial dissection.
The surgeon cuts into the tissue, revealing the capsule of the tumor, which is visible as a distinct layer surrounding the mass.
The surgeon carefully cuts directly adjacent to the tumor, avoiding entry into the tumor itself and preserving the surrounding normal kidney tissue.
The surgeon exposes a well-delineated tumor, clearly visible against the surrounding kidney tissue.
The surgeon gently separates the tumor from the kidney, utilizing the visible capsule as a guide to avoid cutting into normal kidney tissue.
The surgeon exposes the arteries supplying the tumor, which are becoming visible as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon carefully peels the tumor away from the kidney, maintaining a clear separation between the tumor and the normal kidney tissue.
The surgeon continues to manage the large exophytic tumor, which is visibly located on the outer surface of the kidney, facilitating straightforward separation from the kidney tissue.
The surgeon observes bleeding within the capsule surrounding the kidney tumor, a common occurrence during the nephrectomy procedure.
The video shows a small renal tumor with a thick capsule surrounding it, indicating the presence of old hemorrhage or blood products trapped inside.
The surgeon has successfully excised the kidney tumor, which is now completely removed from the surgical site.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to fulgurate the base of the resected area where the large kidney tumor was removed, ensuring hemostasis.
The surgeon performs a sped-up repair of the kidney, focusing on restoring the anatomical structure after tumor removal.
The surgeon observes a broad-based defect in the kidney, which is visible following the removal of a large tumor.
The kidney artery is visibly clamped, preventing blood flow. If the clamp were removed, significant bleeding would occur from the exposed vessels.
The surgeon places sutures in the kidney to control bleeding from the broad-based defect.
The surgeon completes suturing the deep layer of the kidney defect and removes the arterial clamp, resulting in minimal bleeding from the surgical site.
The surgeon performs a repair on the kidney, addressing a broad-based defect visible in the tissue.
Plastic bolsters are visible in the surgical field, likely used to support or protect tissue during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses plastic bolsters to facilitate the placement of sutures, bringing the two sides of the kidney together.
The surgeon places and tightens a plastic suture on the kidney, contributing to the repair process.
The surgeon uses sutures to bring the edges of the kidney close together, following the direction indicated by arrows.
The surgeon uses a plastic piece to prevent tearing of the kidney capsule during suturing, ensuring the edges of the kidney are brought together without damage.
The surgeon uses a plastic piece to distribute pressure over a larger area of the kidney, enhancing the effectiveness of the sutures in bringing the edges of the kidney together.
The surgeon places an additional plastic piece onto the suture, locking it in place to secure the edges of the kidney.
The video shows a large open area on the kidney prior to the repair, highlighting the extent of the exposed tissue before the surgical intervention.
Following the repair, the previously large defect is now visibly reduced to a small area.
The surgeon aligns the edges of the tissue, resulting in the cessation of bleeding at the repair site.
The surgeon transitions from addressing an exophytic mass to a central mass, highlighting the change in the location and type of mass being observed.
The surgeon prepares to clamp a structure, with the clamp positioned near the target tissue in the surgical field.
The renal artery is prominently visible in the surgical field, positioned directly in front of the camera view.
A large mass obscures the view of the kidney, with the spleen visible in the background.
The surgeon clamps the renal artery adjacent to the tumor, preparing for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon has separated the tumor from the renal artery, creating a visible space between the two structures.
The surgeon observes a centimeter of space between the renal artery and the adjacent tumor, highlighting the proximity of these structures during the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon incises the capsule of the kidney, revealing the tumor capsule located directly beneath it.
The surgeon carefully removes additional kidney tissue to avoid penetrating the tumor, due to the absence of a protective capsule.
The surgeon identifies the capsule surrounding the tumor within the kidney and follows it down, minimizing the removal of normal kidney tissue.
The surgeon highlights the proximity of the tumor to the main renal vessels, noting that the tumor is situated less than a centimeter away from these structures.
The surgeon cuts into the kidney tissue, revealing an arterial bleed that becomes visible at the incision site.

The surgeon begins cutting the bottom of the tumor, revealing its connection to the main renal artery. The vessel is visible and tugging slightly as the dissection progresses. The assistant prepares a clip to secure the small artery as it becomes exposed.
A surgical clip is applied to the main vessel supplying the tumor, effectively occluding it.
The surgeon continues to dissect around the tumor, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to expose the nearby renal vessels.
The surgeon follows the contour of the tumor, which is encapsulated in a smooth, round capsule, during the dissection process.
The surgeon focuses on the base of the tumor, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon gently peels away the tumor from surrounding tissues, revealing a smooth separation as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon confirms that the dissection is proceeding without breaching the tumor capsule, ensuring the tumor remains intact during the nephrectomy.
The surgeon exposes the normal kidney tissue located beneath the tumor, highlighting the distinction between the tumor and healthy kidney structures.
The surgeon carefully peels away the tumor from surrounding tissues, ensuring no remnants are left behind as the capsule separates smoothly.
The surgeon accesses the main renal artery, preparing to remove the entire kidney.
The surgeon observes that the kidney is attached to the main renal artery, indicating the need for careful dissection around this vascular structure.
The surgeon identifies several vessels connected to the kidney and proceeds to cut across them, preparing for the removal of the kidney.
The surgeon has clamped the vessels, visibly securing them to prevent blood flow during the nephrectomy procedure.

The surgeon places the excised larger tumor into a containment bag to prevent spillage into the body cavity. The tumor is completely sealed off, and the surgeon proceeds with the repair, suturing a hemostatic agent to the surgical site.
The surgeon places a bolster in the surgical field, likely to support or reinforce the area following vessel cutting and tumor removal.
The surgeon applies Surgicel, a hemostatic agent, to the surgical site to aid in controlling bleeding.
The surgeon applies pressure on the base of the repair site, where the two edges of the tissue were unable to be fully approximated.
The surgeon carefully addresses a broad defect adjacent to the artery, taking precautions to avoid damaging the artery during the repair process.
The surgeon applies a hemostatic agent to the base of the repair site, completing the process.
The surgeon visually assesses a tumor measuring approximately one to one and a half centimeters in size.
The surgeon visually examines a superior mass, which is identified as part of the nephrectomy procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies a tumor located on the superior aspect of the kidney.
The surgeon proceeds with the nephrectomy without clamping the renal artery, allowing continuous arterial blood flow due to the small size of the superior kidney mass.
The surgeon observes a small mass located on the superior aspect of the kidney, ensuring careful dissection to avoid major vessels in the area.
During the resection, a clamp is applied to the bottom left area of the surgical field, exerting pressure near the artery entering the upper pole of the kidney.
The surgeon applies selective clamping to the upper pole of the kidney, focusing on controlling blood flow to this specific area during the nephrectomy.
The surgeon cuts off the small section of the upper pole of the kidney, observing minimal bleeding from the site.
The surgeon performs a nephrectomy without clamping the renal artery, allowing continued blood flow to the rest of the kidney while excising the tumor from the upper pole.
The surgeon performs a superficial repair on a small defect in the kidney, avoiding the need for a deep layer closure.
The surgeon completes the repair of the kidney defect using two sutures, with minimal bleeding observed during the process.
The surgeon places the first suture through the kidney tissue, using a bolster to support the repair of the small defect.
The surgeon tightens the suture, securing two sutures in place on the kidney.
The surgeon completes the repair of the kidney, addressing a small defect with sutures.
The surgeon pulls the edges of the kidney defect together, achieving closure with minimal bleeding, despite the absence of arterial clamping.
The surgeon places a small piece of Surgicel, a hemostatic agent, on the resection site to minimize the risk of postoperative bleeding.
The surgeon removes the instruments, revealing the kidney positioned beneath the liver, which exerts pressure on it.
The surgeon displays the removed adipose tissue that was previously surrounding the kidney.
